channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: It’s a Crowded and Contested World Cup Halftime Show
item.description: Madonna, Justin Bieber, Shakira, BTS and more will perform in a new tournament festivity. Soccer purists aren’t singing praises.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Clockwise from top left: XNY/Star Max and GC Images, via Getty Images; Carl Recine/Getty Images; Owen Sweeney/Invision, via Associated Press; Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The inaugural World Cup halftime show will include, clockwise from top left, Madonna, Burna Boy, Shakira and Justin Bieber.

item.title: What to Know Before Seeing ‘The Odyssey’: Plot, Characters and More
item.description: Christopher Nolan’s adaptation of Homer’s epic poem has monster battles, a sorceress and dozens of thirsty suitors. Here’s a primer.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal Pictures
item.media:description: Matt Damon as Odysseus in the new movie.

item.title: My 5 Favorite Places for Art in Venice
item.description: Our critic Jason Farago shares what you shouldn’t miss in a city full of secrets and surprises.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.category: Palazzo Grimani di Santa Maria Formosa (Venice, Italy, Museum)
item.media:credit: Clockwise from left: Fine Art Images/Heritage Images, via Getty Images; Susan Wright for The New York Times, and Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: Christopher Nolan on ‘The Odyssey’ and IMAX Technical Challenges
item.description: He and the cinematographer Hoyte van Hoytema combined new inventions (like fire-mimicking lights they developed) with century-old techniques.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Erik Tanner for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I called up my friends at IMAX,” Christopher Nolan said. “I couldn’t tell them what the movie was yet, but I said if ever we were going to do a film all in IMAX, this is the one.”

item.title: Has the World Cup Turned Me Into … a Sports Guy?
item.description: The pleasures of watching the TV show of the summer as a bandwagon fan.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: The spectacle of the World Cup, which wraps up on Sunday, stretches far beyond the pitch.

item.title: Lindsay Hubbard Prefers a Long-Distance Boyfriend
item.description: The “Summer House” cast member once yelled at a boyfriend, “How many sandwiches have you made for me?” These days, she’s enjoying her space.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Jamie Mccarthy/Getty Images for Siriusxm

item.title: X and Music Publishers Retreat From Lawsuits
item.description: Elon Musk’s social media platform and the music companies agreed to dismiss dueling claims, from 2023 and 2026. They have not said why.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo/Reuters
item.media:description: Elon Musk’s social media company did not respond to requests for comment on the agreement, which was made public on Friday.

item.title: Blockbuster Summer Movies for the Hottest Summer Ever
item.description: “Iron Man 4: Age of Air Con” coming to theaters? With record-setting heat projected for 2026, our cartoonist imagines a cool slate of films.
item.dc:creator: Michael Lukk Litwak
item.category: Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl (Movie)

item.title: The Rediscovery of Country Original Gary Stewart
item.description: Gary Stewart spent a few years on top, but was largely forgotten after his tragic death in 2003. Now a new generation is rediscovering the onetime “king of honky-tonk.”
item.dc:creator: Bob Mehr

item.title: The Internet’s Steve Lacy, Syd and Matt Martians Discuss New Music
item.description: The band members Steve Lacy and Syd, both of whom have new solo albums out now, reunite with the producer Matt Martians to discuss achieving TikTok fame, writing a Beyoncé song and staying a family.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Joe Coscarelli and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: ‘The Real World’ and Nathan Fielder Can Both Thank Ross McElwee
item.description: Ross McElwee’s documentaries have shaped reality TV, comedy, podcasts and more in ways that you may not realize.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Heartstopper Forever’ Review: Facing a Grown-Up Reality
item.description: The glossy Netflix series graduates into adulthood with this movie starring Kit Connor and Joe Locke as a couple with thoughts of college ahead.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘Heartstopper Forever’: What to Know Before Seeing the Movie
item.description: The Netflix series and its feature-length finale are based on the graphic novels by Alice Oseman. Here’s a reminder of the events of the characters and where their stories left off.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Mark Morris: A Little Bit Country, a Little Bit Jazz
item.description: Morris’s dance troupe presents “Dances to American Music,” three programs at the Joyce Theater.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: Joe Melson, 91, Dies; Wrote ‘Only the Lonely’ and Other Hits With Roy Orbison
item.description: Working together closely, the pair turned out more than 200 songs, including “Crying,” “Running Scared” and “Blue Bayou.”
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: The Surprising Parallels Between ‘The Odyssey’ and ‘Oppenheimer’
item.description: Our movie critic Alissa Wilkinson describes how Christopher Nolan’s version of “The Odyssey” builds on key themes in his previous film “Oppenheimer.”
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Gabriel Blanco

item.title: Brenda Fricker, First Irish Actress to Win an Oscar, Dies at 81
item.description: She won for playing a stoic mother in “My Left Foot” and achieved cult status as a pigeon whisperer in Central Park in “Home Alone 2.”
item.dc:creator: Natasha King
item.category: Home Alone 2: Lost in New York (Movie)

item.title: ‘Obsession’ and Other Horror Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month brings a catastrophic crush, underhanded ushers and a sinister St. Nick.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: The Burning, Bleeding, Breathing Art of Ana Mendieta
item.description: A Tate Modern survey of the artist’s lifetime of work sidesteps chronology to focus on her engagement with nature. But does that give us the whole picture?
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge

item.title: Five Free Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include epics from Werner Herzog and Akira Kurosawa, along with one of the most quintessential New York movies.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu
item.category: The Taking of Pelham One Two Three (Movie)

item.title: 5 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Shifters’ Review: Fast-Forwarding Through a Predictable Romance
item.description: Des (Heather Agyepong) and Dre (Daniel Ezra), navigate a mutual magnetic pull in this British love story by Benedict Lombe at Cherry Lane Theater.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: Want More of the ‘Odyssey’? Read These Books Next.
item.description: Informative histories, modern retellings and genre-bending homages will take you back to the long road to Ithaca.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: Late Night Is Amused by Alleged White House Teleprompter Grift
item.description: Hosts responded on Thursday to a teleprompter operator reportedly winning around $100,000 betting on what President Trump would say in his speeches.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Chloe Fineman Is Leaving ‘S.N.L.’ After 7 Seasons
item.description: The comedian and actress announced her departure as the show looks ahead to its 52nd season.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Colin Cauldwell NBC, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Britney Spears was one of many celebrities portrayed by Chloe Fineman during her time on “Saturday Night Live.”

item.title: Dave Kendall, Creator and Host of MTV’s ‘120 Minutes,’ Dies at 63
item.description: With his English accent and ubiquitous leather jacket, Mr. Kendall was the face of alternative music on MTV from 1989 to 1992.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald
item.media:credit: Steve Eichner
item.media:description: Dave Kendall at Limelight nightclub in New York in 1993. As the host of “120 Minutes” on MTV, he promoted acts like R.E.M., the Red Hot Chili Peppers and Nirvana, who became mainstream stars.

item.title: Isabelle Huppert Speaks Across a Language Divide at the Avignon Festival
item.description: A stripped-back, cross-cultural reading with the Korean star Lee Hye-young at the Avignon Festival brought out a rare softness in the French actress.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle

item.title: Why Netflix’s ‘The Polygamist’ Has Viewers Outside South Africa Talking
item.description: The hit Netflix show follows the life of a wealthy married couple in South Africa as they deal with the pressures of infidelity and appearances.
item.dc:creator: Zimasa Matiwane

item.title: Akihiro Miwa, Actor and Singer With Gender-Fluid Glamour, Dies at 91
item.description: In Japan, he was one of the first androgynous pop-culture stars and an inspirational figure. Abroad, he was known for his work in animated films by Hayao Miyazaki.
item.dc:creator: Ash Wu

item.title: ‘American Pachuco: The Legend of Luis Valdez’ Review: A Rebel With a Cause
item.description: The documentary about the stage and screen director is unabashedly celebratory, and leaves you wanting to see more.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
item.category: American Pachuco: The Legend of Luis Valdez (Movie)

item.title: A Spectacular Theodore Roosevelt Library Deep in the Badlands
item.description: An inventive $450 million center devoted to Roosevelt, who died in 1919, blends in with the natural beauty of North Dakota and celebrates conservation.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Will Warasila
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times

item.title: Ted Huffman Takes Over the Aix-en-Provence Festival
item.description: Ted Huffman, an American director with a collaborative and exploratory spirit, has taken over the Aix-en-Provence Festival in France.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Andrea Mantovani for The New York Times

item.title: Duchamp Gave Up Art for Chess. At MoMA, He Inspires a New Gambit.
item.description: Susan Polgar, a chess grandmaster, will be playing 50 artists, critics and fans of Marcel Duchamp at the same time, in honor of his birthday, July 28.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Solomon

item.title: 9 Art Shows to Catch Before They Close This Summer
item.description: Among some must-see exhibitions are the Whitney Biennial, a Marcel Duchamp retrospective and a sprawling New Museum survey.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:credit: Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo by Jonah Rosenberg for The New York Times
item.media:description: A “Boîte-en-Valise” by Marcel Duchamp.

item.title: At Hudson Valley Shakespeare, a ‘King Lear’ Worth Traveling For
item.description: A beautifully observed version of a man’s final act, along with a lively staging of “As You Like It,” engage the surrounding mountains in conversation.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: ‘They Fight’ Review: It Might Have Been a Contender
item.description: This real-life inspired boxing drama has muscular ambitions, but too little finesse.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Horsegirls’ Review: Finding Her Stride
item.description: An autistic woman finds independence by training for a hobbyhorse competition in this sweet-and-sour coming-of-age film.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘The Kidnapping of Arabella’ Review: Stolen Childhoods
item.description: A disturbed young woman tries to heal her past by abducting a rebellious child in this strange and intermittently touching road movie.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: Finding the Great America, 250 Years On, in 5 Washington Museums
item.description: Six history-rich shows at the Smithsonian Institution are a culturally-representative accounting of this nation’s history, our critic says. See them before they close.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter
item.category: National Museum of African American History and Culture

item.title: Book Review: ‘I Want You to Be Happy,’ by Jem Calder
item.description: Jem Calder’s novel, “I Want You to Be Happy,” follows the flailing situationship between two aspiring writers with a 12-year age gap.
item.dc:creator: Lizzy Harding
item.category: I Want You to Be Happy (Book)
item.media:credit: Or Lapid

item.title: Jimmy Fallon Pokes Fun at Joe Biden’s New Memoir
item.description: “It’s the second book Biden has written,” Fallon said of the former president. “The first was ‘The Odyssey.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: PEN America Defends Israel Article That Led to President’s Resignation
item.description: A co-chief executive of the free expression group said a recent article about Israeli and Jewish writers had been “critical” to pursue.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/Getty Images For Pen America
item.media:description: Clarisse Rosaz Shariyf, left, and Summer Lopez were named co-chief executives of PEN America in February.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Delight to Your Day
item.description: Odysseus’ social network, performative boredom and decorated envelopes.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Scott Bryce, ‘As the World Turns’ Actor, Dies at 68
item.description: He originated the role of the scheming Craig Montgomery and appeared in 280 episodes of the CBS soap opera over more than two decades.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:credit: Robert Voets/CBS, via Everett Collection
item.media:description: Scott Bryce in 2007. He originated the role of Craig Montgomery on “As the World Turns” in 1982 and played him off and on until 2008.

item.title: ‘The Potluck’ Review: The Greensboro Massacre, and a Heavy Legacy to Bear
item.description: César Alvarez’s ambitious new work is a mixed musical meal: It wants to be a call to revolutionary action but is surprisingly lacking in it.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthony Alfaro, center, stars in “The Potluck” as César, the stand-in for the show’s playwright, César Alvarez.

item.title: ‘The Odyssey’ Review: Passion in Every Frame
item.description: Few directors close the divide between the art film and the blockbuster like Christopher Nolan.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘Heartstopper Forever’: Growing Up, and Staying True to Themselves
item.description: Like “Heated Rivalry,” this beloved Netflix romance has a big-hearted ethos. It’s bringing that spirit to a new film that rounds out a teenage TV hit.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Stanford

item.title: ‘Hadestown’ Hits Movie Theaters, and Theater to Stream in July
item.description: Streaming picks include the family-friendly sci-fi adventure “Franklin’s Key” in Philadelphia and “Hamlet” at the Delacorte Theater in Central Park.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman

item.title: Octavia Spencer Believed in Herself Even When Hollywood Didn’t
item.description: It took years for her to be cast as “the main character,” but she considers that the industry’s loss. Her latest series is the action comedy “Ride or Die.”
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Thea Traff
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I wish I could bottle it up and give it to the world,” Spencer said of her self-confidence, “to say you have importance, take up as much space as you want.”

item.title: Robert Wilson’s Watermill Center Without Robert Wilson
item.description: “When I’m no longer living, can this place exist?” Robert Wilson wondered in a film about his Long Island art incubator. This month, its annual festival will go on without him.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Yuvraj Khanna for The New York Times
item.media:description: The choreographer Madeline Hollander, center, with Antuan Byers, left, and Cori Kresge at the Watermill Center, where she is creating a response to “Einstein on the Beach.”

item.title: On ‘Caribenya,’ Lido Pimienta Found a Lifeline
item.description: On Lido Pimienta’s new album, “Caribenya,” the Colombian Canadian musician sought a warmer, more complex depiction of Caribbean culture. Creating it helped her rediscover the beauty of living.
item.dc:creator: Carolina Abbott Galvão
item.media:credit: Tommy Keith for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Pimienta’s new album, images of dragonflies, rivers and the sea abound, as do depictions of fraught emotional relationships and critiques of colonialism.

item.title: Why Did a Major Museum’s Bong Show Go Up in Smoke?
item.description: After the Toledo Museum of Art canceled a pioneering exhibition of glass cannabis pipes, many people wondered what lay behind the decision.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Michelle Gustafson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Golbert has been making glass pipes for 30 years.

item.title: For the Eclipse or a Weekend, How to Save Money in Reykjavik
item.description: A museum pass, public hot springs and free airline stopovers help shrink the cost of visiting Iceland’s capital.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac and Sigga Ella
item.media:credit: Sigga Ella for The New York Times

item.title: Michelangelo’s Forgotten Renaissance Masterpiece
item.description: Tired of the David? A trip across Florence to see the small Santo Spirito Crucifix, a tender vision in old wood, is one of the best decisions you could make in your art-journeying life.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Fine

item.title: Late Night Calls Lindsey Graham’s Senate Seat ‘an Inheritance Jackpot’
item.description: “A Senate seat? Most people just get a garage full of damp boxes,” Michael Kosta said of Graham’s sister filling the late senator’s seat on “The Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: A Soundtrack to the 2026 World Cup Semifinals
item.description: Listen to songs from Rosalía, Mauvais Djo, Ca7riel & Paco Amoroso and the Beatles.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Tomas Cuesta/Getty Images
item.media:description: Fans of the Argentina team celebrating after a win over Switzerland in the World Cup quarterfinals.

item.title: ‘The Whoopi Monologues’ Review: Expanding a One-Woman Show
item.description: Instead of one Whoopi Goldberg, audiences now experience five actresses: Kerry Washington, Kara Young, Dominique Fishback, Danielle Pinnock and Kecia Lewis.
item.dc:creator: Brittani Samuel
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: Kecia Lewis, Dominique Fishback, Danielle Pinnock, Kerry Washington and Kara Young in the play “The Whoopi Monologues” at the Mitzi E. Newhouse Theater.

item.title: At Young World Festival, Rap’s Avant-Garde Takes Shape
item.description: The Brooklyn festival Young World spotlights emergent indie rappers and veteran artists. It was the brainchild of Mike, an artist and label head who has become a fulcrum of the alt-rap scene.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Green and Marcus Maddox
item.media:credit: Marcus Maddox for The New York Times

item.title: With Lizzo and Calm Flair, a Storied Venue Reopens for Summer
item.description: The gorgeously renovated Hunter Pavilion at Ravinia, the Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s off-season home, was inaugurated with a gala last weekend.
item.dc:creator: David Allen
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lizzo performing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra at Ravinia on Saturday.

item.title: T. Rex Fossil Sells for $50.1 Million, Putting the King Back On Top
item.description: In a booming fossil market, the T. rex had been dethroned by a stegosaurus. No longer.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Timothy A. Clary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The 12-and-a-half-foot-tall T. rex, known as Gus, on view at Sotheby’s ahead of its auction on Tuesday.

item.title: In ‘The Westies,’ Chris Brancato Expands His ‘Scumbag Universe’ (Again)
item.description: For his new series, the co-creator of “Narcos” and “Godfather of Harlem” turns his focus to a violent gang of Irish New Yorkers in the early 1980s.
item.dc:creator: Austin Considine and David Billet
item.media:credit: David Billet for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Difficult People’ a Decade Later: A Sarcastic Time Capsule
item.description: This cult comedy about two snide show-business outsiders has a new Blu-ray edition, and a reunion this fall. The creator Julie Klausner discusses why she’d love to revive it.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Egner
item.media:credit: Linda Kallerus/Hulu
item.media:description: Julie Klausner and Billy Eichner starred in “Difficult People,” which ran for three seasons on Hulu. (With Derrick Baskin, background.)

item.title: As White House Leans on Smithsonian, Little Uproar Over Board Vacancies
item.description: An odd silence has surrounded a delay to fill several open seats on the board of the Smithsonian, even as a fierce battle to control the institution ensues.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Alex Wroblewski/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: For more than a year, President Trump has attacked the Smithsonian Institution. Typically, Congress would have confirmed new board nominees by now.

item.title: A Beastie Boy Returns to the Mic, With Some Help
item.description: Thanks to an assist from his sons, Mike D is set to release “Thank You,” the first new music from a member of the pioneering rap trio in 15 years.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and Ryan Lowry
item.media:credit: Ryan Lowry for The New York Times
